title_en: Comprehensive Discourse on Deficiency and Excess
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: What is meant by deficiency and excess? Qibo replied: When the pathogenic qi is exuberant, this is excess; when the essential qi is despoiled, this is deficiency. Huangdi said: What are deficiency and excess like? Qibo said: When qi is deficient, it is the lung that is deficient; when qi rebels upward, the feet become cold. If this occurs out of its proper season, the patient lives; if it occurs in its proper season, the patient dies. The same holds for the other viscera. Huangdi said: What is meant by double excess? Qibo said: What is called double excess refers to a case of great heat disease, where the qi is hot and the pulse is full — this is called double excess. Huangdi said: What of a case where both the channels and the network vessels are excess — how is it treated? Qibo said: When both channels and network vessels are excess, the inch pulse is tense while the cubit pulse is slack; both must be treated. Hence it is said: a slippery pulse indicates a favorable course, a rough pulse an adverse one. Deficiency and excess both follow from the nature of the affected substance; thus when the five viscera, bones, and flesh are smooth and well-ordered, one may enjoy long life. Huangdi said: What of a case where the qi of the network vessels is insufficient while the qi of the channels is surplus? Qibo said: When the network qi is insufficient and the channel qi is surplus, the pulse at the wrist opening is hot while the cubit is cold; this is adverse in autumn and winter, favorable in spring and summer — treat according to the governing disease. Huangdi said: What of a case where the channels are deficient and the network vessels full? Qibo said: When the channels are deficient and the network vessels full, the cubit is hot and full while the wrist-opening pulse is cold and rough; such a patient dies in spring and summer and lives in autumn and winter. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: When the network vessels are full and the channels deficient, moxa the yin and needle the yang; when the channels are full and the network vessels deficient, needle the yin and moxa the yang. Huangdi said: What is meant by double deficiency? Qibo said: When the pulse qi is deficient above and the cubit is deficient, this is called double deficiency. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: What is called deficient qi means the pulse lacks constancy. A deficient cubit means the patient walks with a faltering, unsteady gait. A deficient pulse means it does not resemble what is proper to yin. In such cases, a slippery pulse means life; a rough pulse means death.

Huangdi said: What of a case where cold qi suddenly surges upward and the pulse is full and excess? Qibo said: If excess is accompanied by a slippery pulse, the patient lives; if excess is accompanied by a rebellious pulse, the patient dies. Huangdi said: What of a case where the pulse is excess and full, the hands and feet are cold, and the head is hot? Qibo said: In spring and autumn the patient lives; in winter and summer the patient dies. If the pulse is floating and rough, and this roughness is accompanied by bodily heat, the patient dies. Huangdi said: What of a case where the whole body is swollen and full? Qibo said: When the whole body is full, the pulse is tense, large, and hard, while the cubit is rough and fails to respond; in such a case, a favorable course means life, an adverse course means death. Huangdi said: What is meant by a favorable course meaning life, and an adverse course meaning death? Qibo said: What is called favorable is when the hands and feet are warm. What is called adverse is when the hands and feet are cold. Huangdi said: What of a nursing infant afflicted with heat disease, whose pulse is suspended and small? Qibo said: If the hands and feet are warm, the child lives; if cold, the child dies. Huangdi said: What of a nursing infant struck by wind-heat, panting, wheezing, and heaving the shoulders in breath — what is the pulse like? Qibo said: When panting, wheezing, and heaving the shoulders, the pulse is excess and large; if moderate, the child lives; if tense, the child dies. Huangdi said: What of intestinal flux with blood in the stool? Qibo said: If the body is hot, the patient dies; if cold, the patient lives. Huangdi said: What of intestinal flux discharging white froth? Qibo said: If the pulse is sunken, the patient lives; if floating, the patient dies. Huangdi said: What of intestinal flux discharging pus and blood? Qibo said: If the pulse is suspended and cut off, the patient dies; if slippery and large, the patient lives. Huangdi said: What of cases akin to intestinal flux where the body is not hot and the pulse is not suspended and cut off? Qibo said: If the pulse is slippery and large, this indicates life; if suspended and rough, this indicates death — the time of crisis being determined according to the viscus involved. Huangdi said: What of epilepsy? Qibo said: If the pulse strikes large and slippery, the disease resolves of itself in time; if the pulse is small, hard, and tense, it is fatal and cannot be treated. Huangdi said: In the pulse of epilepsy, what of deficiency and excess? Qibo said: If deficient, it can be treated; if excess, the patient dies. Huangdi said: In wasting-thirst disease, what of deficiency and excess? Qibo said: If the pulse is excess and large, even a disease of long standing can be treated; if the pulse is suspended, small, and hard, a disease of long standing cannot be treated.

Huangdi said: As for the measurement of the body's form, the measurement of the bones, the measurement of the vessels, and the measurement of the sinews — how is one to know these measures? Huangdi said: In spring, one urgently treats the channels and network vessels; in summer, one urgently treats the channel transport points; in autumn, one urgently treats the six bowels; in winter, the pores are sealed shut. When they are sealed shut, one employs medicinal decoctions and uses needle and stone but sparingly. What is meant by using needle and stone sparingly does not apply to abscesses and welling-sores, for abscesses and welling-sores permit no delay of even a moment. When an abscess's location is unclear, so that pressing upon it evokes no response beneath the hand, and it comes and goes suddenly, needle three points beside the Hand Greater Yin channel, and two points each at the tasseled vessels. For a great, hot armpit abscess, needle five points of the Foot Lesser Yang; if after needling the heat does not subside, needle three points of the Hand Ruler of the Heart, and needle three points each at the meeting of the great bone along the Hand Greater Yin channel and its network vessels. For a sudden abscess with the sinews drawn taut, causing pain that follows the divisions of the flesh, with the essence-sweat not fully spent and the qi of the bladder insufficient, treatment lies at the channel transport points. For sudden abdominal fullness that does not yield to pressure, select the network vessel of the Hand Greater Yang, which is the alarm point of the stomach, and the transport point of the Lesser Yin, five fen to the side of the spine, three cun from the vertebrae, using the round, sharp needle. For sudden turmoil disorder, needle five points beside the transport points, and three points at the Foot Yang Brightness and above it to the side. For epilepsy and fright, needle five points of the vessel, needle five points each of the Hand Greater Yin, needle five points of the Greater Yang channel, needle one point beside the network vessel of the Hand Lesser Yin, one point of the Foot Yang Brightness, and needle three needles five cun above the ankle.

In general, when treating wasting-thirst, sudden collapse, hemiplegia, wilting and reversal, and fullness of qi with rebellious ascent — these are diseases of the rich and noble who feast on fine grain and rich meat. Obstruction and blockage in the region of the diaphragm, with upper and lower failing to communicate, are diseases born of sudden excessive worry. Sudden fainting together with deafness, and one-sided blockage and closure without free passage, arise from a sudden clash of the internal qi. Illnesses that arise neither from within nor from wind striking from without are those in which emaciation lingers and settles. Limping and lameness of the foot are diseases of cold, wind, and dampness. Huangdi said: Jaundice, sudden pain, epilepsy, fainting, and mania are generated by prolonged rebellious qi. Disharmony of the five viscera is generated by blockage and closure of the six bowels. Headache and ringing in the ears, with the nine orifices failing to function smoothly, are generated by disorders of the intestines and stomach.

title_en: Discourse on Greater Yin and Yang Brightness
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Greater Yin and Yang Brightness stand in exterior-interior relation, being the vessels of the spleen and stomach; why then do the diseases they generate differ? Qibo replied: Yin and yang occupy different positions; they alternate between deficiency and excess, between rebellion and compliance; some diseases follow from within, others from without — since what they follow differs, the diseases bear different names. Huangdi said: I wish to hear of these differing manifestations. Qibo said: Yang is the qi of Heaven and governs the exterior; yin is the qi of Earth and governs the interior. Hence the way of yang is excess, the way of yin is deficient. Thus when one is struck by marauding wind and depleting evil, yang receives it; when eating and drinking are without moderation and rising and resting are without regularity, yin receives it. When yang receives it, the evil enters the six bowels; when yin receives it, the evil enters the five viscera. Entering the six bowels, the body becomes hot, one cannot rest at proper times, and above there is panting and gasping; entering the five viscera, there is distension, fullness, and blockage, and below there is undigested diarrhea, which in time becomes intestinal flux. Thus the throat governs the qi of Heaven, while the gullet governs the qi of Earth. Hence yang receives the qi of wind, yin receives the qi of dampness. Thus the yin qi travels upward from the feet to the head, then downward along the arms to the fingertips; the yang qi travels upward from the hands to the head, then downward to the feet. Hence it is said: in diseases of yang, the qi ascends to its limit and then descends; in diseases of yin, the qi descends to its limit and then ascends. Thus one injured by wind is affected first above; one injured by dampness is affected first below.

Huangdi said: When the spleen is diseased, the four limbs lose their function — why is this? Qibo said: The four limbs all receive their qi from the stomach, yet this qi cannot reach the channels directly; it must depend upon the spleen in order to be received. Now if the spleen is diseased and cannot circulate the fluids on behalf of the stomach, the four limbs cannot receive the qi of water and grain; the qi daily grows more depleted, the pathways of the vessels become unfree, and the sinews, bones, muscles, and flesh all lack the qi to be nourished and grow — hence they lose their function.

Huangdi said: Why does the spleen not govern a season of its own? Qibo said: The spleen belongs to earth and governs the center; it constantly nourishes the other four viscera throughout the four seasons, being entrusted with governance for eighteen days at the close of each season, and thus cannot alone preside over a single season. The spleen viscus constantly manifests the essence of the stomach's earth qi. Earth gives birth to the myriad things and takes its model from Heaven and Earth; hence its influence reaches upward and downward, to the head and to the feet, and it cannot be confined to governing one season alone.

Huangdi said: The spleen and stomach are joined only by a membrane, yet the spleen is able to circulate the stomach's fluids on its behalf — how is this? Qibo said: The Foot Greater Yin is one of the three yin; its vessel penetrates the stomach, belongs to the spleen, and connects with the gullet — hence Greater Yin circulates qi to the three yin on the stomach's behalf. Yang Brightness constitutes the exterior and is the sea of the five viscera and six bowels; it likewise circulates qi to the three yang. The viscera and bowels each receive qi from Yang Brightness by way of their respective channels; hence the spleen is able to circulate the stomach's fluids on its behalf. Should this fail, the four limbs cannot receive the qi of water and grain, growing daily more depleted; the pathways of yin become unfree, and the sinews, bones, muscles, and flesh lack the qi to be nourished and grow — hence they lose their function.

title_en: Explanation of the Yang Brightness Vessel
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: When the vessel of the Foot Yang Brightness is diseased, the patient comes to dislike people and fire, and startles in alarm on hearing the sound of wood struck, while the sound of bells and drums does not move him — why does the sound of wood alone startle him? I wish to hear the reason for this. Qibo replied: Yang Brightness is the vessel of the stomach, and the stomach belongs to earth; hence one who startles at the sound of wood does so because earth abhors wood. Huangdi said: Good. Why does he come to dislike fire? Qibo said: Yang Brightness governs the flesh, and its vessel is abundant in blood and qi; when evil qi lodges within it, heat results, and when the heat grows severe, the patient comes to dislike fire. Huangdi said: Why does he come to dislike people? Qibo said: When Yang Brightness suffers reversal, the patient pants and feels oppressed at heart; feeling oppressed at heart, he comes to dislike people. Huangdi said: In some cases the panting patient dies, in others the panting patient lives — why is this? Qibo said: When the reversed, counterflowing qi implicates the viscera, the patient dies; when it merely implicates the channels, the patient lives.

Huangdi said: Good. When the disease grows severe, the patient casts off his clothes and runs about, climbs to high places and sings, or goes for several days without eating, scaling walls and climbing onto roofs — reaching places that, in health, would be entirely beyond his ability. Why is it that in disease he is able to do what he ordinarily cannot? Qibo said: The four limbs are the root of all the yang; when yang is exuberant, the four limbs become replete, and being replete, the patient is able to climb to high places. Huangdi said: Why does he cast off his clothes and run about? Qibo said: Heat is exuberant throughout the body, and so he casts off his clothes and wishes to run. Huangdi said: Why does he rave, curse, and revile without regard for kin or stranger, all the while singing? Qibo said: When yang is exuberant, it causes a person to rave, curse, and revile without regard for kin or stranger, and to have no desire to eat; having no desire to eat, he runs about wildly.

Huangdi asked: Now, heat diseases are all of the category of cold damage; some patients recover, others die. Those who die all do so within six or seven days, while those who recover all take more than ten days. I do not understand the reason for this and wish to hear its explanation. Qibo replied: The Greater Yang is the chief of all the yang; its vessel connects with the Wind Mansion point, and therefore it governs the qi of all the yang channels. When a person is injured by cold, heat disease results; though the heat may be severe, the patient does not die. But one who is doubly stricken by cold, affected in both yin and yang at once, cannot escape death.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear what this looks like. Qibo said: On the first day of cold damage, the Greater Yang receives it, and so there is pain in the head and neck and stiffness of the lumbar spine. On the second day, Yang Brightness receives it; Yang Brightness governs the flesh, and its vessel flanks the nose and connects with the eyes, and so the body becomes hot, the eyes ache, the nose grows dry, and the patient cannot rest. On the third day, Lesser Yang receives it; Lesser Yang governs the gallbladder, and its vessel follows the flanks and connects with the ears, and so there is pain in the chest and flanks and deafness. When all three yang channels and their network vessels have received the disease, but it has not yet entered the viscera, the disease may be resolved by inducing sweat. On the fourth day, Greater Yin receives it; the Greater Yin vessel spreads through the stomach and connects with the gullet, and so there is abdominal fullness and dryness of the throat. On the fifth day, Lesser Yin receives it; the Lesser Yin vessel penetrates the kidney and connects with the lung, and links with the root of the tongue, and so the mouth parches, the tongue dries, and there is thirst. On the sixth day, Reverting Yin receives it; the Reverting Yin vessel follows the genitals and connects with the liver, and so there is vexation and fullness and contraction of the scrotum. When the three yin and three yang, and the five viscera and six bowels, have all received the disease, so that the nutritive and defensive qi no longer circulate and the five viscera no longer communicate, then the patient dies.

In one who is not doubly stricken by cold, on the seventh day the Greater Yang disease wanes and the headache somewhat improves; on the eighth day, the Yang Brightness disease wanes and the bodily heat somewhat improves; on the ninth day, the Lesser Yang disease wanes and hearing is faintly restored; on the tenth day, the Greater Yin disease wanes, the abdomen returns to its normal size, and the patient begins to think of food and drink; on the eleventh day, the Lesser Yin disease wanes, the thirst stops, the fullness subsides, the tongue's dryness ends, and the patient sneezes; on the twelfth day, the Reverting Yin disease wanes, the scrotum relaxes and the lower abdomen slightly descends, the pathogenic great qi departs entirely, and the disease resolves day by day. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: In treating it, one opens and frees the vessel corresponding to each viscus in turn, and the disease will wane and resolve day by day. In cases not yet past three days, one may resolve the disease by inducing sweat; in cases past three days, one may resolve it by purgation.

Huangdi said: When heat disease has already resolved, there are cases in which some residue lingers on — why is this? Qibo said: In all such cases of residue, the heat was still severe when the patient was forced to eat, and so a residue was left behind. In such cases the disease has already waned, yet heat still remains stored within; because the qi of the food clashes with it, the two heats combine, and so a residue remains. Huangdi said: Good. How is this residue treated? Qibo said: Examine whether it is deficiency or excess, and regulate what is rebellious and what is compliant, and the disease can certainly be made to resolve. Huangdi said: In heat disease, what should be forbidden? Qibo said: When heat disease has only somewhat improved, eating meat will cause relapse, and eating too much will leave a residue — these are the things to be forbidden.

Huangdi said: In cases doubly stricken by cold, what are the corresponding pulse and clinical signs? Qibo said: In double stricken by cold, on the first day of disease, both the Greater Yang and Lesser Yin are diseased together, causing headache, dry mouth, and vexatious fullness; on the second day, both Yang Brightness and Greater Yin are diseased together, causing abdominal fullness, bodily heat, disinclination to eat, and delirious speech; on the third day, both Lesser Yang and Reverting Yin are diseased together, causing deafness, contraction of the scrotum, and reversal, so that the patient can take neither water nor gruel and no longer recognizes those around him; on the sixth day he dies. Huangdi said: If the five viscera are already injured, the six bowels no longer communicate, and the nutritive and defensive qi no longer circulate, why does death then follow only after a further three days? Qibo said: Yang Brightness is the chief of the twelve channels and vessels, abundant in blood and qi; hence though the patient no longer recognizes others, it takes three more days for its qi to be entirely exhausted, and only then does death follow. In general, when cold damage disease transforms into warm disease: if it occurs before the Summer Solstice, it is called warm disease; if it occurs after the Summer Solstice, it is called summerheat disease. In summerheat disease, sweat should be allowed to flow out freely together with the heat, and should not be checked.

In liver heat disease, the urine first turns yellow, there is abdominal pain, excessive lying down, and bodily heat; when the heat contends within, the patient raves and starts in fright, the flanks are full and painful, the hands and feet are restless, and the patient cannot rest easy; the disease worsens on the days of the stems Geng and Xin, and profuse sweating occurs on the days of Jia and Yi; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Geng or Xin day. Needle the Foot Reverting Yin and Lesser Yang; when there is rebellion, the head aches dizzily, and the pulse draws upward, surging into the head. In heart heat disease, the patient first feels despondent, and only after some days does heat appear; when the heat contends, there is sudden heart pain, vexation and oppression, frequent retching, headache, a red face, and no sweating; the disease worsens on Ren and Gui days, and profuse sweating occurs on Bing and Ding days; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Ren or Gui day. Needle the Hand Lesser Yin and Greater Yang. In spleen heat disease, the head first feels heavy and the cheeks ache, there is vexation of the heart, a greenish complexion, a desire to retch, and bodily heat; when the heat contends, there is lumbar pain that prevents bending forward or backward, abdominal fullness and diarrhea, and pain in both sides of the jaw; the disease worsens on Jia and Yi days, and profuse sweating occurs on Wu and Ji days; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Jia or Yi day. Needle the Foot Greater Yin and Yang Brightness. In lung heat disease, the patient first shivers as if drenched, the fine body hairs stand on end, there is aversion to wind and cold, a yellow coating on the tongue, and bodily heat; when the heat contends, there is panting and coughing, pain that travels through the chest and back so the patient cannot take a deep breath, unbearable headache, and sweating together with chill; the disease worsens on Bing and Ding days, and profuse sweating occurs on Geng and Xin days; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Bing or Ding day. Needle the Hand Greater Yin and Yang Brightness until blood the size of a large bean emerges, and the disease resolves at once. In kidney heat disease, the patient first suffers lumbar pain and aching of the shins, distressing thirst with frequent drinking, and bodily heat; when the heat contends, there is pain and stiffness of the neck, cold and aching of the shins, heat in the soles of the feet, and disinclination to speak; when there is rebellion, the neck aches with a dizzy, wavering sensation; the disease worsens on Wu and Ji days, and profuse sweating occurs on Ren and Gui days; if the qi rebels, the patient dies on a Wu or Ji day. Needle the Foot Lesser Yin and Greater Yang. In every case where sweating is due, it occurs upon the day that overcomes the disease's own phase. In liver heat disease, redness first appears on the left cheek; in heart heat disease, redness first appears on the forehead; in spleen heat disease, redness first appears on the nose; in lung heat disease, redness first appears on the right cheek; in kidney heat disease, redness first appears on the chin. Even before the disease has manifested, if this redness is seen, one should needle at once — this is called treating the disease before it arises. When heat disease arises from the region corresponding to its own phase, it resolves by the appointed time; when one needles contrary to this, it resolves only after three cycles; and where the rebellion is compounded, the patient dies. In every case where sweating is due, it comes forth profusely on the day that overcomes the disease's own phase. In treating heat disease generally, give the patient cold water to drink before needling, dress him in cool clothing, and keep him in a cool place, stopping once the body has cooled.

When heat disease begins with pain in the chest and flanks and restlessness of the hands and feet, needle the Foot Lesser Yang and supplement the Foot Greater Yin; in severe cases, apply the fifty-nine-point needling. When heat disease begins with pain in the hand and arm, needle the Hand Yang Brightness and Greater Yin, and stop when sweating occurs. When heat disease begins in the head, needle the Greater Yang points of the neck, and stop when sweating occurs. When heat disease begins in the foot and shin, needle the Foot Yang Brightness, and stop when sweating occurs. When heat disease begins with heaviness of the body and aching of the bones, deafness, and a fondness for closing the eyes to sleep, needle the Foot Lesser Yin; in severe cases, apply the fifty-nine-point needling. When heat disease begins with dizziness and clouded vision together with heat, and fullness of the chest and flanks, needle the Foot Lesser Yin and Lesser Yang. When the color of the Greater Yang vessel glows at the cheekbone, this is heat disease; if the color has not yet spread to meet the adjoining region, one may say sweat will soon be obtained, and one need only await the proper time for it to resolve. But if this color appears contending together with the color of the Reverting Yin vessel, the time of death is no more than three days away, for the heat disease has penetrated inward to connect with the kidney — this being the color of the Lesser Yang vessel. When the color of the Lesser Yang vessel glows in front of the cheek, this is heat disease; if the color has not yet spread to meet the adjoining region, one may say sweat will soon be obtained, and one need only await the proper time for it to resolve; but if this color appears contending together with the color of the Lesser Yin vessel, the time of death is no more than three days away.

In heat disease, among the qi points: the space below the third vertebra governs heat in the chest; the space below the fourth vertebra governs heat in the diaphragm region; the space below the fifth vertebra governs heat of the liver; the space below the sixth vertebra governs heat of the spleen; the space below the seventh vertebra governs heat of the kidney; and the point for the telltale glow lies at the sacrum. The depression above the neck at the third vertebra corresponds to the center. Where the glow runs from the cheek up counter to the cheekbone, it signifies a great abdominal mass; where it appears below at the jaw, it signifies abdominal fullness; where it appears behind the cheekbone, it signifies pain in the flanks; where it appears on the upper cheek, it signifies involvement above the diaphragm.

Huangdi asked: There is a disease of warmth in which, whenever sweat comes out, the fever promptly returns, the pulse remains agitated and rapid without abating along with the sweat, and the patient raves and cannot eat — what is this disease called? Qibo replied: This disease is called yin-yang crossing; crossing means death. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the explanation. Qibo said: The reason a person sweats is that sweat is always generated from grain, and grain in turn generates essence. Now when the evil qi contends with the essence within the bones and flesh and sweat is thereby obtained, this signifies that the evil has retreated and the essence has prevailed; when the essence prevails, the patient ought to be able to eat and the fever ought not to return. A return of fever indicates the evil qi; sweat indicates the essential qi. Now if sweat comes out and the fever promptly returns, this signifies that the evil has prevailed. Inability to eat means the essence has nothing to replenish it. When the disease lingers on in this way, the patient's life may be overturned in an instant. Moreover, the Discourse on Heat states: if sweat comes out while the pulse remains agitated and exuberant, the patient dies. Now since the pulse fails to correspond to the sweating, this shows the patient cannot overcome the disease, and death is certain. Raving speech signifies the loss of the will; loss of the will means death. Since here we see three signs of death and not a single sign of life, even should the patient seem to improve, he will certainly die.

Huangdi said: There is a disease in which the body is hot, sweat comes out, and there is vexation and fullness, yet the vexation and fullness are not relieved by the sweating — what disease is this? Qibo said: Sweating together with bodily heat is due to wind; sweating with vexation and fullness that is not relieved is due to reversal — this disease is called wind reversal. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the whole of it. Qibo said: The Greater Yang governs the qi and therefore receives the evil first; the Lesser Yin stands with it in exterior-interior relation, and when it encounters this heat, it follows the Greater Yang's qi upward, and this following-upward constitutes the reversal. Huangdi said: How is this treated? Qibo said: Needle both the exterior and interior channels, and give the patient decoctions to drink.

Huangdi said: What manner of disease is taxation-wind? Qibo said: Taxation-wind, as a rule, lodges beneath the lung; as a disease it causes stiffness in the upper back and dimness of vision, with spittle coming forth like mucus, aversion to wind, and shivering with cold — this is the disease of taxation-wind. Huangdi said: How is it treated? Qibo said: Treatment aims to restore the ability to bend and stretch. In one whose Greater Yang still draws upon sufficient essence, it resolves in three days; in a person of middle years, in five days; in one deficient in essence, in seven days, when the patient coughs up greenish-yellow mucus resembling pus, as large as a pellet, coming out through the mouth or nose. If it fails to come out, it will injure the lung, and injury to the lung brings death.

Huangdi said: There is a disease called kidney wind, in which the face becomes puffy and swollen, and speech is impaired — may this be treated with needling? Qibo said: When it is due to deficiency, needling is not appropriate; if one needles when it is not appropriate, then after five days the pathogenic qi is certain to arrive in force. Huangdi said: What is it like when it arrives? Qibo said: When it arrives, the patient certainly suffers shortness of breath and intermittent fever, the fever rising from the chest and back up to the head, with sweating, hot hands, dry mouth, bitter and distressing thirst, yellow urine, swelling beneath the eyes, rumbling in the abdomen, heaviness of the body making walking difficult, cessation of the menses, vexation with inability to eat, and inability to lie flat on the back — for lying flat brings on coughing. This disease is called wind water, and its treatment is discussed among the needling methods. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the explanation. Qibo said: Wherever evil qi gathers, the correct qi there is certainly deficient; when yin is deficient, yang is bound to gather there — hence the shortness of breath, intermittent fever, and sweating. Yellow urine indicates heat within the lower abdomen. Inability to lie flat indicates disharmony within the stomach; when the patient does lie flat, the coughing grows severe, because the qi presses upward upon the lung. In all cases of water qi, a slight swelling appears first beneath the eyes. Huangdi said: On what basis is this said? Qibo said: Water belongs to yin, and the region beneath the eyes likewise belongs to yin; the abdomen is where utmost yin resides. Hence when water lodges in the abdomen, it necessarily causes swelling beneath the eyes as well. When the true qi rebels upward, the mouth turns bitter and the tongue dry, and the patient cannot lie flat; when he does lie flat, he coughs up clear water. In all water diseases, the patient therefore cannot rest lying down; if he does lie down, he startles, and starting brings on severe coughing. Rumbling in the abdomen indicates the disease is rooted in the stomach. When it presses upon the spleen, there is vexation and inability to eat; when food fails to descend, this is obstruction at the stomach duct. Heaviness of the body making walking difficult is because the stomach vessel runs through the feet. Cessation of the menses is due to closure of the uterine vessel; the uterine vessel belongs to the heart and connects with the interior of the uterus. Now, since the qi presses upward upon the lung, the heart qi cannot communicate downward, and so the menses fail to come. Huangdi said: Good.

Huangdi asked: When a person's body is not unusually warm and not unusually hot, yet the patient experiences heat with vexation and fullness — why is this? Qibo replied: This is because the yin qi is diminished while the yang qi prevails; hence there is heat with vexation and fullness.

Huangdi said: When a person's clothing is not cold and there is no cold qi within, yet cold arises from within — why is this? Qibo said: This is a person with abundant impediment qi; the yang qi is diminished and the yin qi abundant, and so the body feels as cold as if just emerged from water.

Huangdi said: There are people whose four limbs are hot, and when they encounter wind and cold, the limbs feel as if scorched by fire — why is this? Qibo said: In such a person, the yin qi is deficient and the yang qi exuberant. The four limbs belong to yang; when yang meets yang, while the yin qi is scant and deficient, the scant water cannot extinguish the exuberant fire, so that yang alone governs. Where yang alone governs, it cannot properly generate and nurture growth — it only prevails alone until it exhausts itself. When such a person encounters wind and the limbs feel as if scorched by fire, this signifies that his flesh is being consumed away.

Huangdi said: There are people whose bodies are cold, whom neither hot broths nor fire can warm, nor thick clothing make warm, and yet they do not shiver from the cold — what disease is this? Qibo said: In such a person, the kidney qi has habitually prevailed, given over to the affairs of water, so that the Greater Yang qi has declined, and the kidney's fat has withered and ceased to grow; a single portion of water cannot overcome two portions of fire. The kidney belongs to water and generates the bones; when the kidney fails to generate, the marrow cannot be filled, and so the cold penetrates severely even into the bones. The reason such a person does not shiver is that the liver is one yang and the heart is a second yang, while the kidney is a solitary viscus; one portion of water cannot overcome two portions of fire, and so the patient cannot shiver. This disease is called bone impediment, and such a person is bound to suffer contracture of the joints.

Huangdi said: There are people whose flesh feels numb and unresponsive, so that even wrapped close in padded clothing it remains numb — what illness is this called? Qibo said: This is when the nutritive qi is deficient while the defensive qi is excess. When the nutritive qi is deficient, there is numbness; when the defensive qi is deficient, there is loss of function. When both nutritive and defensive qi are deficient, there is both numbness and loss of function, though the flesh appears outwardly unchanged. When the person's body and will no longer belong to one another, this is called death.

Huangdi said: There are people with counterflow qi who cannot lie down and whose breathing makes a sound; there are those who cannot lie down yet whose breathing makes no sound; there are those whose rising and resting are as usual yet whose breathing makes a sound; there are those who can lie down and walk about yet pant; there are those who cannot lie down and cannot walk yet pant; and there are those who cannot lie down, and when they do lie down, they pant. What viscus in each case brings this about? I wish to hear the explanation. Qibo said: Those who cannot lie down and whose breathing makes a sound suffer from counterflow of the Yang Brightness. The three yang channels of the foot normally flow downward; now, in counterflow, they flow upward, and so the breath makes a sound. Yang Brightness is the vessel of the stomach, and the stomach is the sea of the six bowels, its qi likewise flowing downward normally; when Yang Brightness rebels and cannot follow its proper path, the patient cannot lie down. As the Lower Canon states: when the stomach is not in harmony, sleep is not peaceful — this is what is meant. As for those whose rising and resting are as usual yet whose breathing makes a sound, this is counterflow in the network vessels of the lung; the network vessels cannot follow the channels in their upward and downward movement, and so the qi lingers in the channel and fails to move on; disease of the network vessels affects a person only mildly, and so rising and resting remain as usual even while the breath makes a sound. As for those who cannot lie down, and who pant when they do lie down, this is due to a visitation of water qi; water flows along with the body fluids, and the kidney is the viscus of water, governing the body fluids, and thus governing both the ability to lie down and the occurrence of panting. Huangdi said: Good.
